claims.claim: For NCA cells, the TR phase exhibited the most critical BVG composition, while for LFP and NMC cells, the venting phase proved more critical, largely due to H2 emissions.
claims.quote: For NCA cells, the TR phase exhibited the most critical BVG composition, while for LFP and NMC cells, the venting phase proved more critical, largely due to H2 emissions.

claims.claim: Su values increase with higher state of charge (SoC) for battery vent gases.
claims.quote: Baird et al. (2020) observed that Su values increase with higher SoC

claims.claim: NCA cells exhibited a maximum Su of 1.0 m/s, higher than LCO cells (0.6 m/s) and LFP cells (0.3 m/s), attributable to higher hydrogen and lower carbon dioxide content in NCA vent gas.
claims.quote: Henriksen et al. (2021b) reported that NCA cells exhibited a maximum Su (1.0 m/s), higher than that of LCO (0.6 m/s) and LFP cells (0.3 m/s). This behaviour is largely attributable to the BVG composition: NCA cells release gases with higher hydrogen and lower carbon dioxide content compared to other cells.

claims.claim: The peak of laminar burning velocity occurs at an equivalent ratio of around 1.0 across all cell types and SoC levels.
claims.quote: the peak of Su occurs at an equivalent ratio (ER) of around 1.0 across all cell types and SoC levels.

claims.claim: Increasing temperature significantly enhances Su across all BVG compositions, with LFP cells attaining a maximum Su of 735 cm/s at 500°C in the TR phase, NMC cells reaching 574 cm/s, and NCA cells peaking at 345 cm/s for the global BVG at 500°C.
claims.quote: Fig. 7 demonstrates that increasing temperature significantly enhances Su across all BVG compositions, with notable differences between chemistries. NCA cells show relatively consistent Su values across BVG compositions, peaking at 345 cm/s for the global BVG at 500°C. In contrast, LFP cells attain a higher maximum Su (735 cm/s, at 500°C) in the TR phase, while NMC cells reach an intermediate Su (574 cm/s) in the same phase.

claims.claim: For NMC cells, the venting phase with high H2 content yields a significantly higher Su (222.6 cm/s) than the TR phase (77.2 cm/s) and global event (59.9 cm/s), with the decline in Su in later phases aligning with increasing CO2 and decreasing H2 percentages.
claims.quote: the venting phase, with its high H2 content, yields significantly higher Su values (222.6 cm/s) than the TR phase (77.2 cm/s) and global event (59.9 cm/s). This decline in Su in later phases aligns with the increase in CO2 and decrease in H2 percentages as the thermal abuse test progresses.

claims.claim: For LFP cells, the TR BVG composition yields the highest Su (105.9 cm/s), followed by the venting BVG (88.2 cm/s) and the global BVG (42.0 cm/s), with the higher Su in the TR phase driven by H2 released at TR onset, while the subsequent increase in CO2 mole fraction tempers the global Su.
claims.quote: the TR BVG composition yields the highest Su (105.9 cm/s), followed by the venting BVG (88.2 cm/s) and the global BVG (42.0 cm/s). These variations are found to be correlated with the H2 mole fraction, as this species is released at the time of TR onset, driving the significantly higher Su in the TR phase. The subsequent increase in CO2 mole fraction from TR onset to its peak tempers the global Su.

claims.claim: For NCA cells at standard conditions, the highest Su value (45.5 cm/s) occurs for the global BVG composition, followed by 44.4 cm/s in the TR phase and 32.5 cm/s in the venting phase, with differences arising from variations in H2 mole fractions across phases.
claims.quote: The results show that the highest Su value (45.5 cm/s) occurs when considering the global composition over the entire event, followed by 44.4 cm/s in the TR phase and 32.5 cm/s in the venting phase. These differences arise from variations in the H2 mole fractions across phases, with higher H2 concentration during the TR and global phases leading to elevated Su values compared to the venting phase.

claims.claim: The maximum temperatures reached during thermal runaway were 562 ± 96°C for NCA, 358 ± 70°C for LFP, and 575 ± 101°C for NMC cells, with NCA and NMC cells reaching maximum pressures surpassing 4 barg and LFP cells showing a lower peak pressure under 2 barg.
claims.quote: the peak temperatures for NCA, LFP, and NMC cells were observed to be 562 ± 96°C, 358 ± 70°C, and 575 ± 101°C, respectively. Additionally... Both NCA and NMC cells exhibit similar maximum pressure values, surpassing 4 barg, while LFP cells show a lower peak pressure, under 2 barg.

claims.claim: For NCA cells, H2 is released near the maximum temperature, whereas for LFP and NMC cells, H2 emission begins earlier during venting, with a significant amount released at the onset of TR.
claims.quote: The three emission profiles differ most significantly in H2 release patterns: for NCA cells, H2 is released near the maximum temperature, whereas for LFP and NMC cells, H2 emission begins earlier, during venting, with a significant amount released at the onset of TR.

claims.claim: For NCA cells, venting aligns with cathode degradation onset leading to release of CO and CO2, while in LFP and NMC cells, venting temperatures remain below the cathode decomposition threshold, releasing only electrolyte and F-compounds.
claims.quote: For NCA, venting aligns with cathode degradation onset, leading to the release of CO and CO2, while in LFP and NMC cells, venting temperatures remain below the cathode decomposition threshold, releasing only electrolyte and F-compounds.

claims.claim: EC emissions are linked to temperatures exceeding 248°C due to EC's higher boiling point, suggesting it only vaporizes after TR onset.
claims.quote: EC emissions are linked to temperatures exceeding 248°C due to EC's higher boiling point, suggesting it only vaporizes after TR onset

claims.claim: The Su values calculated by the proposed mechanism for NCA vent gas compositions showed an average error of 7.7%, compared to 9.7% for GRI-Mech 3.0, when validated against experimental data.
claims.quote: The calculated Su values demonstrate good agreement with experimental results, as evidenced by the average error calculation, which yielded 9.7 % for GRI-Mech 3.0 and 7.7 % for the mechanism proposed in this study.

claims.claim: HF and CO from battery vent gas may reach Immediately Dangerous to Life and Health (IDLH) limits as defined by NIOSH.
claims.quote: the literature (Peng et al., 2020; Ubaldi and Russo, 2024a,b) suggests that HF and CO may reach the Immediately Dangerous to Life and Health (IDLH) limits, as defined by the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH, 2023).

claims.claim: Su values calculated using only averaged global BVG compositions without distinguishing phases show significant discrepancies compared to phase-specific Su maxima, underscoring the necessity of phase-specific gas analysis.
claims.quote: Comparing the Su curves from each phase with existing literature further highlights this necessity, as significant discrepancies emerge between the observed Su maxima and those found in studies based on averaged BVG compositions without distinguishing phases.
